coredata.dc:title: Rapid ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using nitrogen doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Pengfei
Pang, Wen
Ding, Shihui
Wang, Dan
Wei, Xunbin
Gu, Bobo
coredata.dc:description: Cancer severely threatens human health currently, promoting the rapid development of cancer treatment strategies. In addition to cancer therapy, assessment of cancer prognosis, which can evaluate the success with treatment and chances of recovery as well as assist to make subsequent therapeutic schedule, is also remarkably indispensable and important. Conventional technologies can’t provide rapid and highly-sensitive assessment of cancer prognosis at cytological level. Herein, an effective nitrogen doped carbon dots with intrinsic nucleolus-targeting capability and high fluorescence quantum yield are synthesized, characterized and employed for fluorescence imaging of nucleolus, which is closely related to the biological alteration of cancer cell. The cancer prognosis thus can be accurately (limit of detection: 50 nM) and rapidly (5 min) assessed at subcellular organelle level from nucleolar characteristics, which are visualized and analyzed by the captured fluorescence images. Outstanding assessment performance endows the proposed technology with great potential for future clinical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence imaging
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Nucleolus targeting
Assessment of cancer prognosis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro drug evaluation and cytotoxicity assay
Tumor model and in vivo chemotherapy
Ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis
Fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs
Ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Center for Protein Therapeutics, University at Buffalo
National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China ( 61805135 ), Pujiang Talents Program ( 18PJ1405100 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2019YFC1604604 ), Shanghai Jiao Tong University ( ZH2018QNA43 ).

Before performing the ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis, the influence of chemotherapy drugs on nucleolus was studied in vitro by measuring its size and volume. One commercial and clinical chemotherapy drug i.e. camptothecin (CPT), which is a potent topoisomerase inhibitor that interferes with the essential function of topoisomerase in DNA replication, was selected to treat the cancer cells including HeLa and 4T1. After incubated with various concentrations of CPT (0, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 μM) for different incubation time (6, 12 and 24 h), HeLa cells were stained with NCDs and imaged by confocal microscopy as shown in Fig. 5A and Fig. S7, respectively. It is obviously observed that the nucleolar size was gradually decreased with the increment of CPT dose. In order to precisely calculate the nucleolar volume and accurately assess the performance of chemotherapy drug, the three-dimensional images of HeLa cells treated with CPT for different incubation time were reconstructed and the nucleolar volume per cell was calculated and counted as shown in Fig. 5B and Fig. S8. It is clear that the nucleolar volume per HeLa cell decreased progressively within the CPT concentration range from 0 to 5 μM, and there was no distinct discrepancy of nucleolar volume of HeLa cells treated with same CPT dose but different incubation time of 6, 12 and 24 h, indicating that the decrease of nucleolar volume is more likely to be dependent on dose instead of incubation time. Moreover, the nucleolar volume per cell reached a plateau at high CPT dose, but it had significant statistical difference at low CPT dose of 0–1 μM. It means the proposed fluorescence imaging method for assessment of cancer prognosis has a very high sensitivity at low drug dose and the limit of detection of (LOD) could reach 50 nM. Meanwhile, the cell viability, a conventional method to evaluate the cellular response to external stimulus including chemotherapy drug, was tested using CCK-8 kit and compared with the proposed method. As shown in Fig. 5C, the cell viability was decreased slowly at low CPT dose, and even no significant decrement was observed when cell was treated with CPT in the concentration range of 0–1 μM for 6 and 12 h. One possibility to support the cell growth is the stock of ribosome when the function of nucleoli was inhabited by CPT. Thus, longer treatment time or high CPT does was required to exhaust the ribosome or enhance chemotherapy effect and subsequently achieve significant difference of viability, which could be detected using CCK-8 kit. It means the proposed methods could measure slight changes of nucleolus that can't be detected using conventional methods. As different cancer cells would have different drug resistance to the same chemotherapy drug, similar experiment was also performed in 4T1 cells as shown in Fig. S9-S11. The proposed method showed higher sensitivity at high CPT dose and comparable sensitivity at low CPT dose as compared with cell viability test. These findings elucidate that the proposed fluorescence imaging method of nucleolus using NCDs has higher sensitivity to evaluate the chemotherapy drug as compared with the conventional cell viability test, enabling it a reliable method to assess the cancer prognosis.This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (61805135), Pujiang Talents Program (18PJ1405100), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFC1604604), Shanghai Jiao Tong University (ZH2018QNA43).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematics illustrating the synthetic procedure of NCDs and the assessment procedure of cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs. (B) The size distribution of NCDs. (C) The absorption spectra and (D) the excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of NCDs. [NCDs] = 50 μg mL−1.

Confocal imaging of live/fixed HeLa and 4T1 cells treated with NCDs. [NCDs] = 200 μg mL−1, Green channel (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 500–550 nm), Red channel (Ex: 552 nm, Em: 555–625 nm). Scale bar: 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The confocal fluorescence, merged and bright field imaging of HeLa (upper) and 4T1 (lower) cells co-stained with SYTO RNASelect and NCDs. Red arrows indicate nucleolus. (B) The time-sequenced confocal fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells stained with NCDs (left) and SYTO RNASelect (right) after the continuous irradiation using 488 nm laser. (C) Anti-photobleaching evaluation of NCDs and SYTO RNASelect. SYTO RNASelect channel (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 510–540 nm), NCDs channel (Ex: 552 nm, Em: 555–625 nm). [SYTO RNASelect] = 10 μM, [NCDs] = 200 μg mL−1. Scale bar: 10 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Confocal fluorescence, merged and bright field imaging of live, fixed, DNase and RNase treated HeLa cells co-stained with Hoechst 33342 and NCDs. Hoechst 33342 channel (Ex: 405 nm, Em: 415–485 nm), NCDs channel (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 500–610 nm). [NCDs] = 200 μg mL−1, [Hoechst 33342] = 5 μg mL−1, red arrows indicate nucleolus. Scale bar: 10 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) The confocal fluorescence, bright field and merged imaging of NCDs-stained HeLa cells treated with CPT at the dose range of 0–5 μM for 6 h. NCDs channel (Ex: 552 nm, Em: 555–625 nm), [NCDs] = 200 μg mL−1, scale bar: 20 μm. (B) The volume of nucleolus per cell treated with various concentrations of CPT for 6 h represented comparation between two groups, and represented comparation within three groups. Error bar indicates SD (SD: standard deviation, ns: no significant difference. ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01, ∗∗∗P < 0.001, n = 5). (C) The viability of HeLa cells treated with various concentrations of CPT (n = 5).

(A) Body weight and (B) tumor volume variations of mice in control and treatment groups within day 14 (n = 5). (C) The imaging of mice and excised tumors from control (upper) and treatment (lower) groups on day 14. (D) The confocal fluorescence and bright field imaging of NCDs-stained cytological samples from control and treatment group, insets show enlarged images. NCDs channel (Ex: 488 nm, Em: 500–550 nm), [NCDs] = 200 μg mL−1. (E) The imaging of AgNOR-stained cytological samples from control and treatment group. (F) The statistical analysis of nucleolar area per cell of AgNOR and NCDs-stained cytological samples (n = 100). Scale bar: 10 μm. Error bar indicates SD (SD: standard deviation, ∗P < 0.05, ∗∗P < 0.01).

head.ce:title: Rapid ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using nitrogen doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 1954 Huashan Road, Shanghai, 200030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, 1954 Huashan Road, Shanghai 200030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Organic-Inorganic Composites, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomedical Engineering Department, Peking University, Beijing, 100081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Carcinogenesis and Translational Research (Ministry of Education/Beijing), Peking University Cancer Hospital & Institute, Beijing, 100142, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Carcinogenesis and Translational Research (Ministry of Education/Beijing)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Carcinogenesis and Translational Research (Ministry of Education/Beijing), Peking University Cancer Hospital & Institute, Beijing 100142, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Pengfei Jiang and Wen Pang contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cancer severely threatens human health currently, promoting the rapid development of cancer treatment strategies. In addition to cancer therapy, assessment of cancer prognosis, which can evaluate the success with treatment and chances of recovery as well as assist to make subsequent therapeutic schedule, is also remarkably indispensable and important. Conventional technologies can’t provide rapid and highly-sensitive assessment of cancer prognosis at cytological level. Herein, an effective nitrogen doped carbon dots with intrinsic nucleolus-targeting capability and high fluorescence quantum yield are synthesized, characterized and employed for fluorescence imaging of nucleolus, which is closely related to the biological alteration of cancer cell. The cancer prognosis thus can be accurately (limit of detection: 50 nM) and rapidly (5 min) assessed at subcellular organelle level from nucleolar characteristics, which are visualized and analyzed by the captured fluorescence images. Outstanding assessment performance endows the proposed technology with great potential for future clinical research.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has become one great threaten to human health in the last decades due to the increment of environmental pollution. According to latest global cancer data from the World Health Organization (WHO), more than 18.1 million new cases and 9.6 million death cases have been reported worldwide in 2018, meaning that cancer is accounted for one sixth of the world’s annual deaths [1]. In addition to surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, which have been widely used in cancer treatment [2], various newly developed cancer treatment strategies have been proposed and demonstrated, including photodynamic therapy [3,4], photothermal therapy [5,6], gene-therapy [7], and immunotherapy [8], etc. Thus, the survival rate and lifetime of most cancer patients have been significantly increased and extended due to the good therapeutic effect endowed by the cancer treatment strategies mentioned above. In order to correctly estimate the success with treatment and chances of recovery, assessment of cancer prognosis is extremely indispensable and important. Meanwhile the assessment could assist to make subsequent therapeutic schedule, further improving survival rate and lifetime of cancer patients [9].

The remarkable feature of malignant tumors is abnormal glucose metabolization i.e. the “Warburg effect” [10,11], which is the most traditional indicator to assess the cancer prognosis. After injected the tracer 2-fluoro-2-d-deoxyglucose (FDG), the glucose metabolization status can be analyzed using positron emission tomography/computed tomography (PET/CT) [12–14], providing vital information for assessment of cancer prognosis. However, due to the limited resolution, nuclear medical imaging technologies can’t offer pathology information at cytological level, which is of great importance to accurately and intuitively assess the cancer prognosis. In clinical, imaging-guide fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) or surgery were performed to obtain the cytological or histological samples [15]. Once treated with the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) or silver reagent, which can stain argyrophilic nucleolar organizer regions (AgNORs), the cytological and histological samples can be observed under optical microscope and analyzed according to the criteria of prognosis [16]. H&E stain and AgNOR methods could enable pathological assessment of histological and cytological samples respectively, which is essential for the diagnosis and prognosis of most cancers [17–22]. However, these methods also suffer from some limitations. H&E stain of histologic samples only present the blurred nucleolus images, lowering its assessment accuracy [23]. As the marker in AgNORs stain method, the nucleolar organizer region (NOR) has shown great value in assessment of cancer prognosis, but AgNOR method can’t provide the imaging of the integrated nucleolus which is closely related with cancer prognosis, increasing the assessment complexity and lowering its accuracy. Thus, it is urgent and important to explore new biological probes combined with advanced imaging method to further enhance assessment performance of cancer prognosis at cytological level. The latest advances show that nucleolus can response to the changes of cellular growth rate and metabolic activity [24]. Meanwhile, both the up-regulation of nucleolar function and increment of nucleolus size are closely associated with excessive ribosome biogenesis and malignant cell proliferation in cancer cells [25–27], e.g. variations of the major tumor suppressor retinoblastoma (Rb) and p53 pathways can stimulate nucleolar function and enlarge nucleolus [28,29]. The biological alteration of cancer cell would lead to the nucleolar hypertrophy, making nucleolus a more efficient and ideal site for assessment of cancer prognosis [30].

Recently, considerable efforts have been devoted to developing nucleolus imaging probes due to the limited commercial probes. Carbon dots (CDs) [31], aggregation-induced emission (AIEgens) [32], CdS quantum dots [33], graphere quantum dots [34], gold nanoclusters [35] and comaplatin [36], etc., have been designed and synthesized for nucleolus imaging. The developed CDs possesses unique advantages including superior biocompatibility, tunable emission, high fluorescence quantum yield, good water solubility, excellent photobleaching resistance, etc. [37,38], making it a promising fluorescence probe for nucleolus imaging. Moreover, the nucleolus-targeting capability could be improved by optimizing the raw materials to form nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) [39–41].

Here, we proposed and demonstrated a new method for rapid ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs. The NCDs with intrinsic nucleolus-targeting capability and high fluorescence quantum yield were designed and synthesized for fluorescence imaging of nucleolus. Once extracted from mice treated with chemotherapy using FNAC method and subsequently fixed and stained with NCDs, the cytological samples were analyzed using fluorescence imaging method. The cancer prognosis can be assessed from nucleolus size, which was visualized and measured from the captured fluorescence images. As compared with conventional, nuclear medical imaging, H&E and AgNORs stain methods, the proposed method possesses some remarkable advantages including higher sensitivity and faster assessment, endowing it with great potential for future clinical research.

Citric acid (CA) was purchased from BeiJing Chemical Works Co. LTD and ethylenediamine (EDA) was procured from TianJin FuChen Chemical Reagents Factory. DMEM medium, RPMI1640 medium, and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were procured from HyClone. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from ScienCell. Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was purchased from Dojindo. Camptothecin (CPT), SYTO RNASelect, Hoechst 33342, deoxyribonuclease (DNase), ribonuclease (RNase) and diethyl pyrocarbonate (DPEC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The camptothecin sodium was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. LTD. 4T1 (breast cancer cell line of mice) and HeLa (cervical cancer cell line of human) were obtained from ScienCell.

The nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method as described in previous literature [42]. Briefly, NCDs were prepared by refluxing the reaction mixture of 2 g CA and 3 mL EDA i.e. the molar mass ratio of CA/EDA was 1:4.5 approximately. Then the condensation reaction between CA and excess of EDA, which was volatilized during the process of hydrothermal reaction, was performed. Finally, the pure NCDs were collected, air-cooled and grinded without any further purification. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) imaging and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were measured by Talos F200X G2 (Thermo Scientific) and ZETASIZER NANO (Malvern). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by IR spectrometer (NICOLET 6700, Thermo Scientific). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured by AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer (Shimadzu-Kratos). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained by UV1901PC spectrophotometer (Shanghai AOYAN Scientific Instrument Co. LTD) and F-2700 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi), respectively.

The 4T1 and HeLa cells were incubated with RPMI1640 and DMEM medium containing 10% FBS at the condition of 5% CO2 and 37 °C, respectively. For confocal imaging studies: 4T1 and HeLa cells (1 × 106) were seeded into 35 mm confocal dishes and cultured with medium overnight. After fixed with 95% ethanol for 15 min and washed three times with PBS or only washed three times with PBS, the fixed and live cells were incubated with NCDs (200 μg mL−1) for 2 h at the condition of 37 °C and 5% CO2. Finally, the treated cells were processed for imaging with confocal microscope (Leica SP8). For colocalization studies: After fixed with prechilled methanol (−20 °C) for 15 min and subsequently permeabilized with 0.3% Triton X-100 for 5 min, the fixed cells were stained with RNASelect (10 μM) for 30 min, which is followed by washing five times with PBS to remove the residual RNASelect. Finally, the RNASelect stained cells were stained with NCDs (200 μg mL−1) for 30 min and then processed for colocalization studies via confocal microscope (Leica SP8). For nucleolus-targeting mechanism studies: HeLa and 4T1 cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA) for 15 min and washed three times with PBS to remove the residual PFA. Then, the fixed cells were treated with DNase (25 μg mL−1) or RNase (25 μg mL−1) for 1 h and subsequently co-stained with both NCDs (200 μg mL−1) and Hoechst 33342 (5 μg mL−1). Finally, co-stained cell imaging was captured by confocal microscope (Leica SP8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro drug evaluation and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: The HeLa and 4T1 cells were seeded into confocal dishes and cultured with medium overnight. After washed 3 times with PBS, the cells were treated with various concentrations of CPT (0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 μM) for 6 h, 12 h and 24 h, respectively. Then the CPT treated cells were washed three times with PBS and subsequently fixed with 95% ethanol for 15 min and washed three times with PBS. Finally, the cells were processed for fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs as described above and the volume of nucleolus was calculated and counted by Imaris. Each data point of Fig. 5B represented the average nucleolus volume per cell of captured fluorescence image, and five fluorescence images were randomly captured for analysis in each group i.e. n = 5. The CCK-8 kit was utilized for cytotoxicity assay as described in previous literature. Briefly, the 4T1 and HeLa cells of exponential phase (5 × 103 cells per well) were seeded on 96 well plates and incubated with RPMI1640 and DMEM medium supplemented with 10% FBS overnight, respectively. The cells were washed three times with PBS and incubated with fresh medium or medium containing different concentrations of CPT (0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 μM), which were control and test groups, for 6 h, 12 h, and 24 h, respectively. After CPT treatment, the cells were rinsed with PBS and incubated with CCK-8 applicated solution (100 μL/well) for 2 h. The optical density (OD) value of each well at 450 nm was measured by microplate auto-reader. The cell viability ratio was calculated from A450 nm/A450 nm (control).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tumor model and in vivo chemotherapy
ce:section.ce:para: Female BALB/c nude mice (6 weeks old) were purchased from Shanghai Laboratory Animal Center. Hair on the abdomen was removed by depilatory cream prior to the in vivo experiments. To establish the orthotopic model of breast cancer, 4T1 cells (1 × 106) were injected in the mammary gland of female BALB/c nude mice. Once the tumor volume reached about 50 mm3, the mice were randomly divided into two groups i.e. control and treatment groups (n = 5) for in vivo chemotherapy experiment. The mice in treatment group were intratumorally injected with 6 mg kg−1 CPT at day 0 and 1, then the half dose (3 mg kg−1) was administered at day 2 and 3, while mice in control group were injected with the equal volume of PBS. The tumor sizes were measured by a vernier caliper ruler and calculated using the formula (tumor volume = (length × width2)/2). The body weight and tumor size of mice were measured each two day until day 14. All the animal care and experimental procedures were approved by the Bioethics Committee of School of Biomedical Engineering, Shanghai Jiao Tong University and were consistent with regulations for the care and use of experimental animals in China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis
ce:section.ce:para: At day 5 i.e. four days after chemotherapy treatment as described above, the fine needle aspiration experiment was performed to obtain the cytological samples of tumor. The sample obtained by fine needle aspiration cytology method was dropped onto clean glass slides rapidly and spreaded by another slide. Then the cytological samples were fixed in 0.1% DPEC-methanol solution for 15 min. Subsequently, the slide was air-dried at room temperature for 1 min. For AgNOR stain methods: AgNOR working solution (a silver staining reagent for argyrophilic proteins of nucleolus) was incubated with the sample for 15 min in the dark at room temperature, and then rinsed with dH2O for 10 min. Finally, the glass slide containing cytological samples was sealed with resin and processed for optical imaging. For NCDs staining methods: the cytological sample were acquired, fixed and dried as mentioned above. Then the cytological samples were stained with NCDs (200 μg mL−1, solvent is water containing 0.1% DPEC) for 1 min and covered with a coverslip. Finally, the glass slide containing cytological samples stained with NCDs was processed for optical imaging immediately. Each data point of Fig. 6F represents the nucleolus volume per cell, wherein the analyzed cells in each group were randomly selected from the cytological samples of corresponding mice (n = 100).

Imaging analysis: Area of fluorescence signals and volume of nucleolus were calculated and counted by Imaris, and the area of AgNOR were counted by Image J. Data analysis: data presented in this research was shown as average ± SD. The two-tailed paired Student’s t-test was used for statistical analysis of tumor volume between Control and Treatment group. And statistical analysis of other data utilized the two-tailed unpaired Student’s t-test.

The NCDs have been designed and synthesized as fluorescence probe for bioimaging of mitochondria, lysosome et al. [43,44]. However, NCDs with inherent nucleolus-targeting capability and high fluorescence quantum yield for nucleolus imaging is rare. Here, NCDs with inherent nucleolus-targeting capability and high fluorescence quantum yield were proposed and demonstrated for rapid assessment of cancer prognosis. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the NCDs could specifically stain the nucleolus of cancer cells, which has been demonstrated as an important indicator of tumor pathology, enabling evaluating the cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus. The NCDs were prepared via reaction of citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) by refluxing the reaction mixture. The synthesized NCDs were characterized by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement and transmission electron microscope (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1B and Fig. S1, the measured average size was around 1.2 nm. Meanwhile, the XPS and FTIR spectra were also recorded as shown in Fig. S2. The XPS spectrum of NCDs shows the element percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are 75.82%, 13.22% and 10.96%, respectively. And the characteristic peaks of –COOH (3423 cm−1 and 1658 cm−1), –NH (1560 cm−1), and C–NH–C (1145 cm−1) were measured by FTIR spectra of NCDs. Moreover, the zeta potential of NCD was measured as −15 mV, which was possibly due to the presence of –COOH group. The NCDs have a broad absorption range from 300 to 700 nm with a main peak at 350 nm and a shoulder peak at 450 nm as shown in Fig. 1C. The NCDs have excitation-dependent emission as shown Fig. 1D, facilitating multi-channel fluorescence imaging combing with commercial probes. As the fluorescent probe, the fluorescence quantum yield is one key parameter to evaluate its performance. The quantum yield of the synthesized NCDs measured at 350 nm was 42.7%, which guarantees high-quality bioimaging with good signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). Moreover, the ability to resist photobleaching is another important requirement for fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. S3, the absorption of NCDs (50 μg mL−1) almost kept constant under light irradiation (400–700 nm, ∼70 mW cm−2) for 10 min, indicating its superior photostability. Meanwhile, the obtained NCDs exhibited a high stability in physiological media, including PBS, FBS and DMEM, for one week as shown in Fig. S4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the targeting capability and universality, the NCDs were incubated with different cell lines including HeLa (human cervical cancer cell line) and 4T1 (mice breast cancer cell line). After incubating with NCDs (200 μg mL−1) for 2 h, the cells were imaged using confocal microscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 , strong green and weak red fluorescence signals of NCDs were observed in the nucleolus area of live cells, indicating that the NCDs could penetrate into cell and accumulate at nucleoli by direct and rapid movement. And enhanced green and red fluorescence signals with better SNR of NCDs were achieved in the nucleolus area of fixed cells, which were treated with 95% ethanol for 15 min and then incubated with NCDs (200 μg mL−1) for 10 min. The enhancement could be explained that the fix procedure efficiently improved the cell membrane permeability and consequently made more NCDs diffuse and accumulate in the nucleolus area. These results showed the nucleolus-targeting capability and universality of the synthesized NCDs.

To further confirm the intracellular localization of NCDs, SYTO RNASelect (one commercial fluorescent probe for nucleolus imaging) was selected to co-stain the fixed HeLa and 4T1 cells with NCDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the fluorescence signal of NCDs completely overlapped with that of SYTO RNASelect, confirming the nucleolus-targeting capability of NCDs. Meanwhile, the subcellular imaging performance of NCDs was also compared with one commercial nucleus-targeting dye i.e. Hoechst 33342. As shown in Fig. 4
, the Hoechst 33342 could stain the whole nucleus except nucleoli in both live and fixed HeLa cells; while the NCDs could specifically light-up the nucleoli, which were indicated in the bright images, in both live and fixed HeLa cells. These results indicate the highly specific nucleolus-targeting capability of NCDs.

The NCDs have showed superior photostability in solution, its intracellular photostability was studied and compared with the SYTO RNASelect. After fixed and incubated with NCDs and SYTO RNASelect separately, the HeLa cells were continuously irradiated with 488 nm laser and imaged using confocal microscopy. The initial fluorescence intensity was used for normalization, and the percentage of fluorescence intensity after additional irradiation was calculated. As shown in Fig. 3B and C, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs located in the nucleoli was still bright (fluorescence loss < 15%) even after 60 s irradiation, while the fluorescence loss of SYTO RNASelect was more than 70% under the same irradiation, confirming the superior intracellular photostability of NCDs.

It is well known that most RNAs (especially the ribosomal RNA) accumulate in the nucleolus, which also contains some DNAs and proteins. In order to study the nucleolus-targeting mechanism of NCDs, the ribonuclease RNase and DNase were selected to hydrolyze RNA and DNA. After hydrolyzing the DNA and RNA by DNase and RNase respectively, the HeLa cells were incubated with Hoechst 33342 and NCDs for targeting capability investigation. As shown in Fig. 4, when the cells were digested by DNase, the whole nucleoli and whole nucleus except nucleoli were still stained by NCDs and Hoechst 33342, respectively. Once the cells were digested by RNase, both NCDs and Hoechst 33342 would stain the whole nucleus. The same phenomenon was also observed in 4T1 cells as shown in Fig. S5. It can be inferred that NCDs could prefer to target RNA rather than DNA, endowing NCDs highly specific nucleolus-targeting capability. To further study the nucleolus-targeting mechanism of NCDs, the fluorescence response of NCDs and SYTO RNASelect to RNA were measured as shown in Figs. S6A–6B . Both NCDs and SYTO RNASelect have “light-switch” effect of nucleic acid. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs (542 nm) and SYTO RNASelect (538 nm) in the prescence of RNA were remarkably enhanced as compared with that of free NCDs and SYTO RNASelect, respectively. This phenomenon might be ascribed to the structural diversity of nucleic acids determining the specific interaction between NCDs and nucleic acids. The NCDs seem to have the similar RNA selectivity and “turn-on” fluorescence response after RNA binding as SYTO RNASelect. Considering that both NCDs and RNA are negatively charged, the interaction of NCDs and RNA i.e. nucleolus-targeting mechanism may not be the electrostatic attraction interaction but may be attributed to the specific surface chemistry (especially the contributions from the carbon source) of the NCDs.

The nucleolus is the factory of ribosome to generate rRNA, which is one extremely important matter for cell growth. As compared with normal cells, malignant cells suffer from uncontrolled and frequently rapid proliferation, leading to the hypertrophic and irregularly shaped nucleoli. Thus, nucleolar morphology of cancer cell, especially the nucleolus size, is an important biomarker of tumor pathology. Currently, cancer therapy is mainly realized by inhabiting the malignant proliferation of cancer cells via chemotherapy, radiotherapy, photodynamic therapy, etc. Here, chemotherapy is selected as the model to study the assessment performance of cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs.

Before performing the ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis, the influence of chemotherapy drugs on nucleolus was studied in vitro by measuring its size and volume. One commercial and clinical chemotherapy drug i.e. camptothecin (CPT), which is a potent topoisomerase inhibitor that interferes with the essential function of topoisomerase in DNA replication, was selected to treat the cancer cells including HeLa and 4T1. After incubated with various concentrations of CPT (0, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 μM) for different incubation time (6, 12 and 24 h), HeLa cells were stained with NCDs and imaged by confocal microscopy as shown in Fig. 5A and Fig. S7, respectively. It is obviously observed that the nucleolar size was gradually decreased with the increment of CPT dose. In order to precisely calculate the nucleolar volume and accurately assess the performance of chemotherapy drug, the three-dimensional images of HeLa cells treated with CPT for different incubation time were reconstructed and the nucleolar volume per cell was calculated and counted as shown in Fig. 5B and Fig. S8. It is clear that the nucleolar volume per HeLa cell decreased progressively within the CPT concentration range from 0 to 5 μM, and there was no distinct discrepancy of nucleolar volume of HeLa cells treated with same CPT dose but different incubation time of 6, 12 and 24 h, indicating that the decrease of nucleolar volume is more likely to be dependent on dose instead of incubation time. Moreover, the nucleolar volume per cell reached a plateau at high CPT dose, but it had significant statistical difference at low CPT dose of 0–1 μM. It means the proposed fluorescence imaging method for assessment of cancer prognosis has a very high sensitivity at low drug dose and the limit of detection of (LOD) could reach 50 nM. Meanwhile, the cell viability, a conventional method to evaluate the cellular response to external stimulus including chemotherapy drug, was tested using CCK-8 kit and compared with the proposed method. As shown in Fig. 5C, the cell viability was decreased slowly at low CPT dose, and even no significant decrement was observed when cell was treated with CPT in the concentration range of 0–1 μM for 6 and 12 h. One possibility to support the cell growth is the stock of ribosome when the function of nucleoli was inhabited by CPT. Thus, longer treatment time or high CPT does was required to exhaust the ribosome or enhance chemotherapy effect and subsequently achieve significant difference of viability, which could be detected using CCK-8 kit. It means the proposed methods could measure slight changes of nucleolus that can’t be detected using conventional methods. As different cancer cells would have different drug resistance to the same chemotherapy drug, similar experiment was also performed in 4T1 cells as shown in Fig. S9-S11. The proposed method showed higher sensitivity at high CPT dose and comparable sensitivity at low CPT dose as compared with cell viability test. These findings elucidate that the proposed fluorescence imaging method of nucleolus using NCDs has higher sensitivity to evaluate the chemotherapy drug as compared with the conventional cell viability test, enabling it a reliable method to assess the cancer prognosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis
ce:section.ce:para: In vitro results showed the potential of fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs for drug evaluation. Considering its highly effective and sensitive, fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs is expected to be feasible and effective for ex vivo assessment of cancer prognosis. Then the orthotopic model of breast cancer was established, and the tumor-bearing mice were treated with CPT or equal volume of PBS as treatment or control groups (n = 5) when the volume of tumor reached about 50 mm3. The body weight and tumor size of mice were measured each two day until day 14. As shown as Fig. 6A, the body weight of mice in treatment group mice was decreased at day 2 to day 4, which may be ascribed to the side-effect induced by chemotherapy, then increased rapidly to normal level. Compared with that of mice in control group, the tumors of mice in treatment group were repressed slightly due to the finiteness of anti-tumor effect of chemotherapy as shown in Fig. 6B–C.

Argyrophilic nucleolar organizer regions (AgNORs) containing a set of argyrophilic proteins can be selectively identified by sliver staining, i.e. AgNOR method, a clinical imaging method of AgNORs for pathology analysis. Thus, AgNOR method was also applied to assess cancer prognosis. After in vivo chemotherapy, the fine needle aspiration was performed on the chemotherapy treated mice to acquire the cytological samples of tumor on day 5, which is followed by the nucleolus or AgNORs imaging of the sample stained with NCDs (200 μg mL−1) or sliver staining reagent, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6D–E. NCDs and sliver staining reagent could specifically and efficiently stain the nucleolus and AgNORs in both control and treatment groups. As similar as the AgNORs changes measured by AgNOR method, the nucleolus size in treatment group was smaller than that in control group due to the chemotherapy effect, making assessment of cancer prognosis by nucleolus size feasible and effective. Meanwhile, fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs showed much better SNR than AgNOR method since noise signal from blood cells, interstitial fluid, etc., which were obviously displayed in bright field imaging, was efficiently eliminated in the proposed technology. Moreover, nucleolus morphology measured by fluorescence imaging using NCDs showed more distinct difference between control and treatment groups as compared with that measured by AgNOR method, indicating that the proposed technology possesses better sensitivity than conventional clinical methods. In order to quantitatively analyze the difference between the proposed methods and conventional clinical methods, the nucleolar area per cell stained with sliver staining reagent and NCDs were measured and analyzed by Image J and Imaris, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6F, the nucleolar area per cell, including sliver staining reagent and NCDs stained cells, in treatment group were statistically smaller than that of control group. Meanwhile, the variation of nucleolar area per cell before and after chemotherapy, measured by fluorescence imaging method, was significantly larger than that measured by conventional clinical method i.e. AgNOR method, confirming that the proposed technology has better sensitivity. Furthermore, the nucleolar area measured by fluorescence imaging using NCDs were much larger than that measured by AgNOR method due to that the AgNOR method only can selectively stain the argyrophilic protein of nucleolus rather than whole nucleolus, thus the proposed fluorescence imaging method of nucleolus using NCDs can show the nucleolar morphology more accurately and thus possess higher sensitivity. Moreover, the fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs can be performed within 1 min once the cytological samples are ready. The total assessment procedure only takes 5 min, which is much rapider than AgNOR method. It is worth to note that the assessment time could be further shortened if advanced analysis software is explored and integrated with the fluorescence imaging system. The proposed fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs shows rapid and highly-sensitive assessment of cancer prognosis, endowing it great potential in further clinical applications.

In summary, we designed and synthesized novel NCDs with intrinsic nucleolus-targeting capability, high fluorescence quantum yield, and excellent photostability. Considering that the nucleolus is a new biomarker related to the tumor pathology, the developed NCDs were used as the contrast agent to show the nucleolus variation before and after chemotherapy via fluorescence imaging method, enabling that fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs is feasible and effective in assessment of cancer prognosis at cytological level. As compared with conventional clinical methods, the proposed technology offers excellent in vitro and ex vivo performance, including high sensitivity, rapidness, etc. This study highlights the great potential of assessment of cancer prognosis by fluorescence imaging of nucleolus using NCDs for future clinical research.

Pengfei Jiang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Wen Pang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Shihui Ding: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Dan Wang: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xunbin Wei: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Bobo Gu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (61805135), Pujiang Talents Program (18PJ1405100), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFC1604604), Shanghai Jiao Tong University (ZH2018QNA43).
